content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 18, 1976

Mr. Charles Drescher
Assistant Director
Community Analysis Bureau
City Hall East
200 No. Main St., Room 1110
Los Angeles, California 90012

Dear Mr. Drescher:

Thank you for your letter of August 5, 1976, requesting informal advice concerning your reporting requirements under the Conflict of Interest Code for the Los Angeles Mayor's Office. Specifically, you ask:

1. Does the disclosure statement require a listing of all clients whose gross bookings with the agency are in excess of $10,000, or should the listings be based upon aggregate commissions relating to particular clients?

2. Does the disclosure statement require listing all airlines, cruise lines, or tour operators from whom income (in the form of deducted commissions) is $10,000 or more?

I appreciated the opportunity to discuss with you in more detail the operations of your retail travel agency. You indicated to me that you were licensed both by the Air Transport Conference, a national organization that deals with domestic air travel, and by the International Air Transport Association, an international body that deals with international air travel. Although you receive a flat commission rate on all air travel tickets sold, you stated that you are not a broker, nor do you have a broker's license. As you explained to me, you purchase individual travel tickets on request, and buy blocks of tickets only when a specific organization or company requests that you purchase a block of tickets for their use. You do not buy blocks of tickets for yourself and then attempt to sell them. Essentially, whether dealing with air tickets, tours, or cruises, you first deduct your commission and then pay the balance received to an area bank that distributes the money to the appropriate carrier (in the case of air travel) or to the tour or cruise operator. When

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE UNIT [illegible]

Mr. Charles Drescher
August 18, 1976
Page Two


booking hotel reservations that are not prepaid, your clients
pay the hotel directly and the hotel subsequently remits your
commission to you. When hotel reservations are prepaid, you
deduct your commission and forward the balance to the hotel.

On the basis of the above facts and the facts contained
in your letter:

     1.  You are not required to disclose all clients whose
         gross bookings with the agency are in excess of
         $10,000; rather, your disclosure should be based
         upon aggregate commissions received from each
         client during the reporting period.

     2.  You are not required to list all airlines, cruise
         lines, or tour operators from whom income (in the
         form of deducted commission) is $10,000 or more,
         since this would result in duplicative reporting
         of income.

     I believe you understand that your income disclosure obli-
gations will depend upon what information is required to be
disclosed under the category to which you are assigned by your
agency. We offer no opinion relative to what breadth of
income disclosure should be in your particular category. Whether
it is appropriate for you to disclose income from a retail travel
agency that you and your wife jointly own is an issue to be deter-
mined by your agency and its code reviewing body after reviewing
the duties and responsibilities of your particular position.
Assuming that such disclosure is appropriate, then it also is
appropriate for you to disclose the name of every client from
whom you received a commission or commissions aggregating
$10,000 or more during a calendar year.

     I hope that these informal comments will be of help to you
in clarifying your reporting obligation under the Mayor's Con-
flict of Interest Code. Please let me know if I can be of any
further assistance.

                                             Sincerely,

                                             [signature]
                                             Edgar A. Kerry
                                             Staff Attorney
                                             Conflicts of Interest
                                             Division
                                             916/322-6444

EAK:gb

ROBERT E JOYCE
DIRECTOR

JULES MERSEL, MGR.
DATA ANALYSIS
DIVISION

CHARLES DRESCHER, MGR.
PROGRAM DEVELOPMENT
DIVISION

DANIEL J O'CONNOR, MGR.
ADMINISTRATIVE SERVICES
DIVISION

CITY OF LOS ANGELES
CALIFORNIA

OFFICE OF THE MAYOR
COMMUNITY ANALYSIS
BUREAU
CITY HALL EAST
200 N MAIN STREET, ROOM 1110
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90012
TELEPHONE 485-2952

TOM BRADLEY
MAYOR

August 5, 1976


Mr. Delbert Spurlock
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California  95814

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

After some discussion with a representative of the Los Angeles
City Attorney's Office, I am still somewhat uncertain about the
established conflict of interest reporting requirements. I am
writing this letter in order to obtain some informal advice with
regard to these requirements.

My position with the City of Los Angeles is that of Assistant
Director of the Community Analysis Bureau, Office of the Mayor,
and Manager of the Program Development Division of that Bureau.
The function of the Bureau is to provide detailed demographic
analyses of the City as it relates to the causes of urban blight
and the suggestion of alternative programs to alleviate these
conditions. The results of these studies are provided to the
U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development, the Mayor and
the City Council. Therefore, the Bureau is in a position to
influence community development decisions.

My question has to do with the following section of the Mayor's
Office Conflict of Interest Code:

"Section 302 - Contents of Statements of Financial
Interest - Income.

- 2 -

B. When income of a business entity, including income
of a sole proprietorship, is required to be reported
under this Code, the statement shall contain:

(1) The name, address, and a general description of
the business activity of the business entity;

(2) In the case of a business entity which provides
legal or brokerage services, the name of every
person who paid fees to the business entity, if
the filer's pro rata share of fees from such person
was equal to or greater than one thousand dollars
($1,000);

(3) In the case of a business entity not covered by
paragraph (2), the name of every person from which
the business entity received payments if the
filer's pro rata share of gross receipts from such
person was equal to or greater than ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) during a calendar year."

Specifically, I own, jointly with my wife, a retail travel agency.
The travel agency, being federally regulated, must abide by rules
and regulations established by the Federal government regarding
commission, discounts, etc.

In the normal course of business, a client will purchase a tour,
cruise or airline ticket. Payments for these tours, cruises, or
tickets are then forwarded to the concerned operator or area bank.
Airline payments are disbursed through an area bank and not paid
from the agency; airline commissions are deducted and retained by
the agency.

With this background information, I would appreciate some informal
advice with regard to the following:

(1) Does the disclosure statement require a listing of
all clients whose gross bookings with the agency
are in excess of $10,000, or should the listings
be based upon aggregate commissions relating to
particular clients?

(2) Does the disclosure statement require listing all
airlines, cruise lines or tour operators from whom
income (in the form of deducted commissions) is
$10,000 or more?

- 3 -

In regard to Question (2), it seems that this would be a duplication
of reporting under Question (1). Further, answers to both Question
(1) and Question (2) would somewhat compromise the viability of
the travel agency.

Thank you for your assistance in resolving these matters.


Very truly yours,

[Signature: Charles Drescher]

CHARLES DRESCHER
Assistant Director


CD:sw

sections.question:
(1) Does the disclosure statement require a listing of all clients whose gross bookings with the agency are in excess of $10,000, or should the listings be based upon aggregate commissions relating to particular clients?
(2) Does the disclosure statement require listing all airlines, cruise lines or tour operators from whom income (in the form of deducted commissions) is $10,000 or more?
sections.conclusion: You are not required to list all clients whose bookings are in excess of $10,000; rather, the listing requirement applies only to those persons from whom the agency received gross receipts equal to or greater than $10,000. In regard to your question regarding tour operators, those commissions directly received by you as the owner are required to be reported under Section 302(3) only if the gross receipts from a particular operator are $10,000 or more during a calendar year. We believe it is appropriate for you to list only those persons from whom you directly received commissions of $10,000 or more.
sections.question_synthetic: Mr. Drescher asks whether conflict of interest disclosure statements for his jointly-owned retail travel agency should list clients based on gross bookings exceeding $10,000 or based on commissions, and whether he must separately list airlines, cruise lines, and tour operators generating $10,000 or more in commissions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that disclosure is required only for clients from whom the travel agency received gross receipts of $10,000 or more, not based on total client bookings. For tour operators and airlines, reporting is required only for those from whom commissions directly received equal or exceed $10,000 during the calendar year.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document appears to be complete with both incoming request and FPPC response. Some text degradation in document scans makes certain words unclear, but main Q/A sections are readable. Response dated August 5, 1976 from FPPC to Mr. Drescher's inquiry dated April 18, 1976.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) Does the disclosure statement require a listing of all clients whose gross bookings with the agency are in excess of $10,000, or should the listings be based upon aggregate commissions relating to particular clients?
(2) Does the disclosure statement require listing all airlines, cruise lines or tour operators from whom income (in the form of deducted commissions) is $10,000 or more?

CONCLUSION: You are not required to list all clients whose bookings are in excess of $10,000; rather, the listing requirement applies only to those persons from whom the agency received gross receipts equal to or greater than $10,000. In regard to your question regarding tour operators, those commissions directly received by you as the owner are required to be reported under Section 302(3) only if the gross receipts from a particular operator are $10,000 or more during a calendar year. We believe it is appropriate for you to list only those persons from whom you directly received commissions of $10,000 or more.

embedding.summary: Mr. Charles Drescher, an Assistant Director with the City of Los Angeles who co-owns a retail travel agency, sought FPPC advice on conflict of interest reporting requirements under the Mayor's Office Conflict of Interest Code, specifically regarding how to report business income from travel agency clients and operators.
